greek_text: Ὑμεῖς ἐστε τὸ φῶς τοῦ κόσμου. οὐ δύναται πόλις κρυβῆναι ἐπάνω ὄρους κειμένη· οὐδὲ καίουσιν λύχνον καὶ τιθέασιν αὐτὸν ὑπὸ τὸν μόδιον ἀλλ’ ἐπὶ τὴν λυχνίαν, καὶ λάμπει πᾶσιν τοῖς ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ. οὕτως λαμψάτω τὸ φῶς ὑμῶν ἔμπροσθεν τῶν ἀνθρώπων, ὅπως ἴδωσιν ὑμῶν τὰ καλὰ ἔργα καὶ δοξάσωσιν τὸν πατέρα ὑμῶν τὸν ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss would be highly skeptical here, treating elevated self-referential claims like this as theological development from the later church rather than the historical Jesus's own words.
Käsemann's dissimilarity criterion makes this kind of claim doubtful, since it closely matches the church's own later confession about Jesus.

Sanders was cautious about explicit self-declarations like this, preferring to infer Jesus's self-understanding indirectly from his actions, such as the Temple incident and choice of twelve disciples.
Allison would ask whether an implicit sense of extraordinary vocation recurs across independent traditions, rather than resting weight on this one explicit formulation.

pericope_theme: Fulfilling the Law
early_manuscript_tradition_general: Early witnesses include P1, P64/67, P77, Codex Sinaiticus, Codex Vaticanus (general gospel-level attestation; verify verse-specific coverage via CSNTM)
gospel_parallels: No established parallel identified — verify against a gospel synopsis (e.g. Aland's Synopsis or Gospel Parallels)
greek_text: Μὴ νομίσητε ὅτι ἦλθον καταλῦσαι τὸν νόμον ἢ τοὺς προφήτας· οὐκ ἦλθον καταλῦσαι ἀλλὰ πληρῶσαι· ἀμὴν γὰρ λέγω ὑμῖν, ἕως ἂν παρέλθῃ ὁ οὐρανὸς καὶ ἡ γῆ, ἰῶτα ἓν ἢ μία κεραία οὐ μὴ παρέλθῃ ἀπὸ τοῦ νόμου, ἕως ἂν πάντα γένηται. ὃς ἐὰν οὖν λύσῃ μίαν τῶν ἐντολῶν τούτων τῶν ἐλαχίστων καὶ διδάξῃ οὕτως τοὺς ἀνθρώπους, ἐλάχιστος κληθήσεται ἐν τῇ βασιλείᾳ τῶν οὐρανῶν· ὃς δ’ ἂν ποιήσῃ καὶ διδάξῃ, οὗτος μέγας κληθήσεται ἐν τῇ βασιλείᾳ τῶν οὐρανῶν. λέγω γὰρ ὑμῖν ὅτι ἐὰν μὴ περισσεύσῃ ὑμῶν ἡ δικαιοσύνη πλεῖον τῶν γραμματέων καὶ Φαρισαίων, οὐ μὴ εἰσέλθητε εἰς τὴν βασιλείαν τῶν οὐρανῶν.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss's rationalizing lens would likely treat the underlying legal dispute as historical, stripped of any supernatural or apologetic embellishment added later.
Käsemann would find this promising if Jesus's position here cuts against both standard Jewish legal practice and later church accommodation, satisfying his dissimilarity test.
Sanders would insist this controversy be read as a debate within, not against, first-century Judaism, over how the law should be applied rather than whether it applied.
Allison would look for this same posture toward the law recurring across multiple, independent controversy scenes before treating it as a secure part of the historical picture.

greek_text: Πάλιν ἠκούσατε ὅτι ἐρρέθη τοῖς ἀρχαίοις· Οὐκ ἐπιορκήσεις, ἀποδώσεις δὲ τῷ κυρίῳ τοὺς ὅρκους σου. ἐγὼ δὲ λέγω ὑμῖν μὴ ὀμόσαι ὅλως· μήτε ἐν τῷ οὐρανῷ, ὅτι θρόνος ἐστὶν τοῦ θεοῦ· μήτε ἐν τῇ γῇ, ὅτι ὑποπόδιόν ἐστιν τῶν ποδῶν αὐτοῦ· μήτε εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα, ὅτι πόλις ἐστὶν τοῦ μεγάλου βασιλέως· μήτε ἐν τῇ κεφαλῇ σου ὀμόσῃς, ὅτι οὐ δύνασαι μίαν τρίχα λευκὴν ποιῆσαι ἢ μέλαιναν. ἔστω δὲ ὁ λόγος ὑμῶν ναὶ ναί, οὒ οὔ· τὸ δὲ περισσὸν τούτων ἐκ τοῦ πονηροῦ ἐστιν.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss's rationalizing lens would likely treat the underlying legal dispute as historical, stripped of any supernatural or apologetic embellishment added later.
Käsemann would find this promising if Jesus's position here cuts against both standard Jewish legal practice and later church accommodation, satisfying his dissimilarity test.
Sanders would insist this controversy be read as a debate within, not against, first-century Judaism, over how the law should be applied rather than whether it applied.
Allison would look for this same posture toward the law recurring across multiple, independent controversy scenes before treating it as a secure part of the historical picture.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss would treat apocalyptic imagery like this as mythological language that later gathered around a more modest original prediction or warning.
Käsemann, following Bultmann's broader skepticism here, would be cautious about specific apocalyptic timetables, though he allowed for an authentic core sense of imminent crisis.
Sanders considered Jewish restoration eschatology central to the historical Jesus, and would treat sayings like this as consistent with, not incidental to, his core message.
Allison, himself a strong advocate for an apocalyptic Jesus, would treat the recurring theme of imminent divine action across sources as one of the most secure parts of the historical picture.

greek_text: Τότε ἤρξατο ὀνειδίζειν τὰς πόλεις ἐν αἷς ἐγένοντο αἱ πλεῖσται δυνάμεις αὐτοῦ, ὅτι οὐ μετενόησαν· Οὐαί σοι, Χοραζίν· οὐαί σοι, Βηθσαϊδά· ὅτι εἰ ἐν Τύρῳ καὶ Σιδῶνι ἐγένοντο αἱ δυνάμεις αἱ γενόμεναι ἐν ὑμῖν, πάλαι ἂν ἐν σάκκῳ καὶ σποδῷ μετενόησαν. πλὴν λέγω ὑμῖν, Τύρῳ καὶ Σιδῶνι ἀνεκτότερον ἔσται ἐν ἡμέρᾳ κρίσεως ἢ ὑμῖν. καὶ σύ, Καφαρναούμ, μὴ ἕως οὐρανοῦ ὑψωθήσῃ; ἕως ᾅδου καταβήσῃ· ὅτι εἰ ἐν Σοδόμοις ἐγενήθησαν αἱ δυνάμεις αἱ γενόμεναι ἐν σοί, ἔμεινεν ἂν μέχρι τῆς σήμερον. πλὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι γῇ Σοδόμων ἀνεκτότερον ἔσται ἐν ἡμέρᾳ κρίσεως ἢ σοί.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss would view the polemical 'woe' form as a plausible authentic rhetorical device, while remaining skeptical of any miraculous elements attached to its context.
Käsemann's criterion favors sayings this confrontational, since such sharp conflict with religious authorities is unlikely to have been invented by a later, more accommodating church.
Sanders would read these woes as an intra-Jewish prophetic critique, not as Jesus breaking from Judaism, and would weigh them against his broader picture of Jesus's conflict with Temple authorities.
Allison would ask whether this kind of prophetic denunciation recurs as a stable memory-pattern across sources, rather than resting the case on this one saying's precise wording.

greek_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς· Οὐκ ἀνέγνωτε τί ἐποίησεν Δαυὶδ ὅτε ἐπείνασεν καὶ οἱ μετ’ αὐτοῦ; πῶς εἰσῆλθεν εἰς τὸν οἶκον τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ τοὺς ἄρτους τῆς προθέσεως ἔφαγον, ὃ οὐκ ἐξὸν ἦν αὐτῷ φαγεῖν οὐδὲ τοῖς μετ’ αὐτοῦ, εἰ μὴ τοῖς ἱερεῦσιν μόνοις; ἢ οὐκ ἀνέγνωτε ἐν τῷ νόμῳ ὅτι τοῖς σάββασιν οἱ ἱερεῖς ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ τὸ σάββατον βεβηλοῦσιν καὶ ἀναίτιοί εἰσιν; λέγω δὲ ὑμῖν ὅτι τοῦ ἱεροῦ μεῖζόν ἐστιν ὧδε. εἰ δὲ ἐγνώκειτε τί ἐστιν· Ἔλεος θέλω καὶ οὐ θυσίαν, οὐκ ἂν κατεδικάσατε τοὺς ἀναιτίους. κύριος γάρ ἐστιν τοῦ σαββάτου ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss's rationalizing lens would likely treat the underlying legal dispute as historical, stripped of any supernatural or apologetic embellishment added later.
Käsemann would find this promising if Jesus's position here cuts against both standard Jewish legal practice and later church accommodation, satisfying his dissimilarity test.
Sanders would insist this controversy be read as a debate within, not against, first-century Judaism, over how the law should be applied rather than whether it applied.
Allison would look for this same posture toward the law recurring across multiple, independent controversy scenes before treating it as a secure part of the historical picture.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss would accept the broad historical outline of suffering and death, while treating any predictive precision in Jesus's own words about it as written after the fact.

Sanders considered a real expectation of conflict and possible death plausible for Jesus, given his prophetic actions in Jerusalem, without requiring the precise predictive wording to be original.
Allison would look for a stable memory of anticipated suffering recurring across sources, treating exact predictive detail as less securely original than the general expectation itself.

greek_text: λέγων· Ἐπὶ τῆς Μωϋσέως καθέδρας ἐκάθισαν οἱ γραμματεῖς καὶ οἱ Φαρισαῖοι. πάντα οὖν ὅσα ἐὰν εἴπωσιν ὑμῖν ποιήσατε καὶ τηρεῖτε, κατὰ δὲ τὰ ἔργα αὐτῶν μὴ ποιεῖτε, λέγουσιν γὰρ καὶ οὐ ποιοῦσιν. δεσμεύουσιν δὲ φορτία βαρέα καὶ ἐπιτιθέασιν ἐπὶ τοὺς ὤμους τῶν ἀνθρώπων, αὐτοὶ δὲ τῷ δακτύλῳ αὐτῶν οὐ θέλουσιν κινῆσαι αὐτά. πάντα δὲ τὰ ἔργα αὐτῶν ποιοῦσιν πρὸς τὸ θεαθῆναι τοῖς ἀνθρώποις· πλατύνουσι γὰρ τὰ φυλακτήρια αὐτῶν καὶ μεγαλύνουσι τὰ κράσπεδα, φιλοῦσι δὲ τὴν πρωτοκλισίαν ἐν τοῖς δείπνοις καὶ τὰς πρωτοκαθεδρίας ἐν ταῖς συναγωγαῖς καὶ τοὺς ἀσπασμοὺς ἐν ταῖς ἀγοραῖς καὶ καλεῖσθαι ὑπὸ τῶν ἀνθρώπων· Ῥαββί.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss would view the polemical 'woe' form as a plausible authentic rhetorical device, while remaining skeptical of any miraculous elements attached to its context.
Käsemann's criterion favors sayings this confrontational, since such sharp conflict with religious authorities is unlikely to have been invented by a later, more accommodating church.
Sanders would read these woes as an intra-Jewish prophetic critique, not as Jesus breaking from Judaism, and would weigh them against his broader picture of Jesus's conflict with Temple authorities.
Allison would ask whether this kind of prophetic denunciation recurs as a stable memory-pattern across sources, rather than resting the case on this one saying's precise wording.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss would treat apocalyptic imagery like this as mythological language that later gathered around a more modest original prediction or warning.
Käsemann, following Bultmann's broader skepticism here, would be cautious about specific apocalyptic timetables, though he allowed for an authentic core sense of imminent crisis.
Sanders considered Jewish restoration eschatology central to the historical Jesus, and would treat sayings like this as consistent with, not incidental to, his core message.
Allison, himself a strong advocate for an apocalyptic Jesus, would treat the recurring theme of imminent divine action across sources as one of the most secure parts of the historical picture.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss would treat apocalyptic imagery like this as mythological language that later gathered around a more modest original prediction or warning.

Sanders considered Jewish restoration eschatology central to the historical Jesus, and would treat sayings like this as consistent with, not incidental to, his core message.
Allison, himself a strong advocate for an apocalyptic Jesus, would treat the recurring theme of imminent divine action across sources as one of the most secure parts of the historical picture.

greek_text: καὶ θεραπεύετε τοὺς ἐν αὐτῇ ἀσθενεῖς, καὶ λέγετε αὐτοῖς· Ἤγγικεν ἐφ’ ὑμᾶς ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ θεοῦ. εἰς ἣν δ’ ἂν πόλιν εἰσέλθητε καὶ μὴ δέχωνται ὑμᾶς, ἐξελθόντες εἰς τὰς πλατείας αὐτῆς εἴπατε· Καὶ τὸν κονιορτὸν τὸν κολληθέντα ἡμῖν ἐκ τῆς πόλεως ὑμῶν εἰς τοὺς πόδας ἀπομασσόμεθα ὑμῖν· πλὴν τοῦτο γινώσκετε ὅτι ἤγγικεν ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ θεοῦ. λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι Σοδόμοις ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐκείνῃ ἀνεκτότερον ἔσται ἢ τῇ πόλει ἐκείνῃ.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss would treat apocalyptic imagery like this as mythological language that later gathered around a more modest original prediction or warning.
Käsemann, following Bultmann's broader skepticism here, would be cautious about specific apocalyptic timetables, though he allowed for an authentic core sense of imminent crisis.
Sanders considered Jewish restoration eschatology central to the historical Jesus, and would treat sayings like this as consistent with, not incidental to, his core message.
Allison, himself a strong advocate for an apocalyptic Jesus, would treat the recurring theme of imminent divine action across sources as one of the most secure parts of the historical picture.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss's method would look past any miraculous or supernatural coloring in the parable's frame and treat the core narrative image as a plausible piece of authentic teaching.

greek_text: Οὐδεὶς λύχνον ἅψας εἰς κρύπτην τίθησιν οὐδὲ ὑπὸ τὸν μόδιον ἀλλ’ ἐπὶ τὴν λυχνίαν, ἵνα οἱ εἰσπορευόμενοι τὸ φέγγος βλέπωσιν. ὁ λύχνος τοῦ σώματός ἐστιν ὁ ὀφθαλμός σου. ὅταν ὁ ὀφθαλμός σου ἁπλοῦς ᾖ, καὶ ὅλον τὸ σῶμά σου φωτεινόν ἐστιν· ἐπὰν δὲ πονηρὸς ᾖ, καὶ τὸ σῶμά σου σκοτεινόν. σκόπει οὖν μὴ τὸ φῶς τὸ ἐν σοὶ σκότος ἐστίν. εἰ οὖν τὸ σῶμά σου ὅλον φωτεινόν, μὴ ἔχον μέρος τι σκοτεινόν, ἔσται φωτεινὸν ὅλον ὡς ὅταν ὁ λύχνος τῇ ἀστραπῇ φωτίζῃ σε.

greek_text: εἶπεν δὲ ὁ κύριος πρὸς αὐτόν· Νῦν ὑμεῖς οἱ Φαρισαῖοι τὸ ἔξωθεν τοῦ ποτηρίου καὶ τοῦ πίνακος καθαρίζετε, τὸ δὲ ἔσωθεν ὑμῶν γέμει ἁρπαγῆς καὶ πονηρίας. ἄφρονες, οὐχ ὁ ποιήσας τὸ ἔξωθεν καὶ τὸ ἔσωθεν ἐποίησεν; πλὴν τὰ ἐνόντα δότε ἐλεημοσύνην, καὶ ἰδοὺ πάντα καθαρὰ ὑμῖν ἐστιν. Ἀλλὰ οὐαὶ ὑμῖν τοῖς Φαρισαίοις, ὅτι ἀποδεκατοῦτε τὸ ἡδύοσμον καὶ τὸ πήγανον καὶ πᾶν λάχανον, καὶ παρέρχεσθε τὴν κρίσιν καὶ τὴν ἀγάπην τοῦ θεοῦ· ταῦτα δὲ ἔδει ποιῆσαι κἀκεῖνα μὴ παρεῖναι. οὐαὶ ὑμῖν τοῖς Φαρισαίοις, ὅτι ἀγαπᾶτε τὴν πρωτοκαθεδρίαν ἐν ταῖς συναγωγαῖς καὶ τοὺς ἀσπασμοὺς ἐν ταῖς ἀγοραῖς. οὐαὶ ὑμῖν, ὅτι ἐστὲ ὡς τὰ μνημεῖα τὰ ἄδηλα, καὶ οἱ ἄνθρωποι οἱ περιπατοῦντες ἐπάνω οὐκ οἴδασιν.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss would view the polemical 'woe' form as a plausible authentic rhetorical device, while remaining skeptical of any miraculous elements attached to its context.
Käsemann's criterion favors sayings this confrontational, since such sharp conflict with religious authorities is unlikely to have been invented by a later, more accommodating church.
Sanders would read these woes as an intra-Jewish prophetic critique, not as Jesus breaking from Judaism, and would weigh them against his broader picture of Jesus's conflict with Temple authorities.
Allison would ask whether this kind of prophetic denunciation recurs as a stable memory-pattern across sources, rather than resting the case on this one saying's precise wording.

greek_text: καὶ ἐγὼ ὑμῖν λέγω, ἑαυτοῖς ποιήσατε φίλους ἐκ τοῦ μαμωνᾶ τῆς ἀδικίας, ἵνα ὅταν ἐκλίπῃ δέξωνται ὑμᾶς εἰς τὰς αἰωνίους σκηνάς. Ὁ πιστὸς ἐν ἐλαχίστῳ καὶ ἐν πολλῷ πιστός ἐστιν, καὶ ὁ ἐν ἐλαχίστῳ ἄδικος καὶ ἐν πολλῷ ἄδικός ἐστιν. εἰ οὖν ἐν τῷ ἀδίκῳ μαμωνᾷ πιστοὶ οὐκ ἐγένεσθε, τὸ ἀληθινὸν τίς ὑμῖν πιστεύσει; καὶ εἰ ἐν τῷ ἀλλοτρίῳ πιστοὶ οὐκ ἐγένεσθε, τὸ ὑμέτερον τίς δώσει ὑμῖν; οὐδεὶς οἰκέτης δύναται δυσὶ κυρίοις δουλεύειν· ἢ γὰρ τὸν ἕνα μισήσει καὶ τὸν ἕτερον ἀγαπήσει, ἢ ἑνὸς ἀνθέξεται καὶ τοῦ ἑτέρου καταφρονήσει. οὐ δύνασθε θεῷ δουλεύειν καὶ μαμωνᾷ.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss would treat apocalyptic imagery like this as mythological language that later gathered around a more modest original prediction or warning.
Käsemann, following Bultmann's broader skepticism here, would be cautious about specific apocalyptic timetables, though he allowed for an authentic core sense of imminent crisis.
Sanders considered Jewish restoration eschatology central to the historical Jesus, and would treat sayings like this as consistent with, not incidental to, his core message.
Allison, himself a strong advocate for an apocalyptic Jesus, would treat the recurring theme of imminent divine action across sources as one of the most secure parts of the historical picture.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss would treat apocalyptic imagery like this as mythological language that later gathered around a more modest original prediction or warning.
Käsemann, following Bultmann's broader skepticism here, would be cautious about specific apocalyptic timetables, though he allowed for an authentic core sense of imminent crisis.
Sanders considered Jewish restoration eschatology central to the historical Jesus, and would treat sayings like this as consistent with, not incidental to, his core message.
Allison, himself a strong advocate for an apocalyptic Jesus, would treat the recurring theme of imminent divine action across sources as one of the most secure parts of the historical picture.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss would treat apocalyptic imagery like this as mythological language that later gathered around a more modest original prediction or warning.
Käsemann, following Bultmann's broader skepticism here, would be cautious about specific apocalyptic timetables, though he allowed for an authentic core sense of imminent crisis.
Sanders considered Jewish restoration eschatology central to the historical Jesus, and would treat sayings like this as consistent with, not incidental to, his core message.
Allison, himself a strong advocate for an apocalyptic Jesus, would treat the recurring theme of imminent divine action across sources as one of the most secure parts of the historical picture.

greek_text: Ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν, ὁ μὴ εἰσερχόμενος διὰ τῆς θύρας εἰς τὴν αὐλὴν τῶν προβάτων ἀλλὰ ἀναβαίνων ἀλλαχόθεν ἐκεῖνος κλέπτης ἐστὶν καὶ λῃστής· ὁ δὲ εἰσερχόμενος διὰ τῆς θύρας ποιμήν ἐστιν τῶν προβάτων. τούτῳ ὁ θυρωρὸς ἀνοίγει, καὶ τὰ πρόβατα τῆς φωνῆς αὐτοῦ ἀκούει καὶ τὰ ἴδια πρόβατα φωνεῖ κατ’ ὄνομα καὶ ἐξάγει αὐτά. ὅταν τὰ ἴδια πάντα ἐκβάλῃ, ἔμπροσθεν αὐτῶν πορεύεται, καὶ τὰ πρόβατα αὐτῷ ἀκολουθεῖ, ὅτι οἴδασιν τὴν φωνὴν αὐτοῦ· ἀλλοτρίῳ δὲ οὐ μὴ ἀκολουθήσουσιν ἀλλὰ φεύξονται ἀπ’ αὐτοῦ, ὅτι οὐκ οἴδασι τῶν ἀλλοτρίων τὴν φωνήν. ταύτην τὴν παροιμίαν εἶπεν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς· ἐκεῖνοι δὲ οὐκ ἔγνωσαν τίνα ἦν ἃ ἐλάλει αὐτοῖς.

quest_perspectives.view: Strauss's method would look past any miraculous or supernatural coloring in the parable's frame and treat the core narrative image as a plausible piece of authentic teaching.
Käsemann's approach favors many parables as authentic precisely because their startling, often subversive logic is distinct from both Jewish parable tradition and the early church's allegorizing.
Sanders would situate this parable within Jewish expectations about God's coming reign, reading it as commentary on restoration rather than abstract spiritual truth.
Allison would note how parables cluster around recurring, memorable images across the tradition, and weigh that pattern more than any single telling's precise wording.

Allison, himself a strong advocate for an apocalyptic Jesus, would treat the recurring theme of imminent divine action across sources as one of the most secure parts of the historical picture.

Allison would look for a stable memory of anticipated suffering recurring across sources, treating exact predictive detail as less securely original than the general expectation itself.
